name: Rangi
description: Rangi, also known as Ranginui, is the Sky Father in Māori mythology, a fundamental figure in the creation narrative of many Polynesian cultures. He is the primordial husband of Papa, the Earth Mother, and their eternal embrace initially kept their children, the gods, confined in darkness between them. This claustrophobic existence prompted the children to contemplate separating their parents, a decision fraught with cosmic consequences. Rangi's tears, shed in sorrow and longing for Papa after their separation, are said to be the origin of rain. He represents the celestial realm, the source of light and storms, and embodies the concepts of fatherhood, authority, and the vast expanse of the heavens. His continued presence above Papa serves as a constant reminder of the primordial unity and the sacrifice made to bring light and life to the world. He is a powerful symbol of the enduring bond between sky and earth, a relationship that sustains all life.
subtitle: Sky Father

keyMyths.title: The Separation of Rangi and Papa
keyMyths.description: The most significant myth involving Rangi is the separation of him from Papa. Trapped in eternal embrace, their children, including Tāne Mahuta (god of the forest), longed for light and space. After much debate, Tāne used his immense strength to push Rangi upwards, separating him from Papa. This act brought light to the world, but also caused immense pain and sorrow to both parents, with Rangi's tears forming the rain. The separation allowed life to flourish but also established a permanent division between the celestial and terrestrial realms.
keyMyths.source: Grey, George. *Polynesian Mythology*. Whitcombe and Tombs, 1956.

keyMyths.title: Rangi's Lament
keyMyths.description: After being separated from Papa, Rangi constantly weeps for his lost love. His tears fall as rain, a constant reminder of the primordial unity and the sacrifice made for the sake of his children. This myth highlights the enduring bond between Rangi and Papa, even in separation. The rain also nourishes the earth, connecting the sky and the land in a continuous cycle of life and sustenance. This cyclical relationship symbolizes the interconnectedness of all things in the Polynesian worldview.
keyMyths.source: Orbell, Margaret. *The Concise Encyclopedia of Māori Myth and Legend*. Canterbury University Press, 1998.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Stars
keyMyths.description: In some traditions, the stars are considered to be the offspring of Rangi and Papa, scattered across Rangi's body after their separation. These stars serve as navigational guides for sailors and represent the vastness and beauty of the celestial realm. They are also seen as ancestral spirits, watching over the world below. The placement of the stars is not random, but rather a deliberate act that reflects the order and balance of the cosmos. Each star holds a specific significance and connection to the earth.
keyMyths.source: Tregear, Edward. *Maori-Polynesian Comparative Dictionary*. Lyon and Blair, 1891.

sources.author: Grey, George

sources.text: A foundational text compiling various Polynesian myths, including detailed accounts of Rangi and Papa.

sources.text: A comprehensive encyclopedia providing concise summaries and interpretations of Māori myths and legends.

sources.text: A dictionary that explores the linguistic connections between Māori and other Polynesian languages, shedding light on the shared origins of their mythology.

associations.name: Sky
associations.significance: Rangi is the Sky Father, representing the vast expanse above.

associations.name: Blue
associations.significance: Associated with the sky and heavens, symbolizing Rangi's domain.

associations.name: Air
associations.significance: As the Sky Father, Rangi is connected to the air and atmosphere.

associations.name: Fatherhood
associations.significance: Represents the paternal figure and provider in Polynesian cosmology.

associations.name: Heavens
associations.significance: Embodies the celestial realm and the dwelling place of gods.

associations.name: Separation
associations.significance: His separation from Papa is central to the creation narrative.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: While direct worship of Rangi is not extensively documented, offerings to other gods may indirectly honor him as the Sky Father. Specific details vary between islands and tribes.

cultural.modernLegacy: Rangi remains a significant figure in Māori and Polynesian identity, featuring prominently in art, literature, and cultural performances. The creation story of Rangi and Papa continues to inspire and inform contemporary understandings of the natural world and ancestral connections.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: In the beginning, Rangi and Papa existed in a close embrace, a primal union that shut out the light and confined their numerous children to the dark space between them. Rangi, the Sky Father, and Papa, the Earth Mother, were locked together in an eternal embrace. Their offspring, who were major gods in their own right, lived in the cramped darkness between them. This state of affairs persisted for an immeasurable time, with the children growing increasingly frustrated by their lack of space and light. The children of Rangi and Papa yearned for the world of light, and debated what to do to escape their dark confinement. This predicament set the stage for one of the most important acts in Polynesian mythology: the separation of the Sky Father and Earth Mother.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: The central myth involving Rangi is the separation of himself from Papa, the Earth Mother. The children of Rangi and Papa, including Tāne (god of forests), Tangaroa (god of the sea), and Tūmatauenga (god of war), grew weary of living in the darkness between their parents. They plotted to separate their parents to bring light into the world. After several failed attempts by other siblings, Tāne Mahuta, god of the forest, used his strong legs to push Rangi upwards, thus separating him from Papa. This separation brought light and space into the world, allowing life to flourish. Rangi, grieved by the separation, continues to weep, his tears forming rain. Papa, also in mourning, sends forth mists to reach her beloved Rangi. This story explains the origins of the sky, earth, and all living things.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: The story of Rangi and Papa has profound and lasting influence on Polynesian cultures. It serves as a foundational narrative for understanding the origins of the universe, the relationship between humans and the natural world, and the interconnectedness of all things. The separation of Rangi and Papa symbolizes the transition from a state of primordial unity to a world of diversity and change. The constant presence of Rangi's tears (rain) and Papa's sighs (mist) serves as a reminder of their enduring love and the ongoing connection between the sky and the earth. The tale continues to inspire artists, writers, and performers, and it is a central element in the preservation and transmission of Polynesian cultural heritage.

shortDescription: Rangi, also known as Ranginui, is the Sky Father in Māori mythology. His separation from Papa, the Earth Mother, is a key event in Polynesian creation narratives.
